title: How To Work With AI Systems
summary: Build a durable mental model for how AI responds, where it fails, and how to work with it safely in everyday knowledge work.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This opening lesson establishes the mental model that the rest of the module depends on before the learner changes any prompts or workflows.
lessons.title: A Working Mental Model
lessons.subtitle: Understand prediction, pattern matching, and why that changes how you prompt.
lessons.objective: Explain what an AI assistant is doing well enough to choose better requests and better review habits.
lessons.useWhen: Understand prediction, pattern matching, and why that changes how you prompt.
lessons.whyItMatters: People over-trust AI when they treat it like a database and under-use it when they treat it like a toy. A practical mental model gives you the useful middle ground.

coreConcepts.title: Prediction, Not Recall
coreConcepts.text: Most AI assistants generate the next useful token based on patterns. That makes them flexible for drafting, summarizing, and restructuring work, but unreliable as an unverified source of truth.

coreConcepts.title: Context Shapes Output
coreConcepts.text: The model only sees the instructions, examples, and source material you provide in the current interaction. Better context almost always beats clever phrasing.

coreConcepts.title: Judgment Stays Human
coreConcepts.text: Your job is to define the task, supply the right material, and review the output for fit, facts, and consequences.

workedExample.scenario: A team lead asks AI to write a customer update with no context and gets a generic, overly confident email.
workedExample.approach: They retry with the audience, decision status, approved facts, and tone requirements.
workedExample.result: The second draft is close to usable because the model is predicting from the right context instead of filling gaps with generic business language.
workedExample.verification: The lead checks dates, ownership, and any promises before sending.

artifact.label: Checklist
artifact.title: Before you ask AI for help
items: State what must not be changed.

doThisNow.task: Take one recurring writing or analysis task and rewrite your prompt around the real deliverable.
steps: Write the exact output you need.
List the facts or source notes the model must use.
Add one sentence describing what a good result should sound like.

commonMistakes: Asking for output before defining the job.
Treating a fluent answer as a verified answer.

roleVariants.manager: Frame AI requests around a deliverable, risk level, and reviewer before you ask for output.
roleVariants.writer: Use AI as a drafting and editing partner, then review for voice, facts, and audience fit.
roleVariants.analyst: Treat AI as a hypothesis generator and explainer, not as a trusted source of record.
roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

lessons.sequenceRationale: Once the learner understands the model, this lesson shows how to shape output quality with better inputs, source material, and constraints.
lessons.title: Inputs, Context, and Constraints
lessons.subtitle: Most quality problems begin with missing context.
lessons.objective: Learn how to feed AI the information it needs without drowning it in irrelevant detail.
lessons.useWhen: Most quality problems begin with missing context.
lessons.whyItMatters: The fastest way to improve results is usually not a smarter model. It is clearer source material, sharper constraints, and better examples.

coreConcepts.title: Inputs Beat Instructions Alone
coreConcepts.text: A request like "summarize this for executives" works best when the relevant document, decision context, and audience needs are present.

coreConcepts.title: Constraints Improve Quality
coreConcepts.text: Word count, format, tone, exclusions, and approval boundaries narrow the solution space and reduce cleanup work.

coreConcepts.title: Relevant Context Only
coreConcepts.text: Include what changes the answer. Remove background that does not affect the deliverable or the model will spend attention on the wrong things.

workedExample.scenario: An operations manager asks AI to draft a process update from memory.
workedExample.approach: They provide the old SOP, the actual policy change, and the intended audience.
workedExample.result: The revision preserves what matters and updates only the sections that changed.
workedExample.verification: The manager compares the output against the source policy and flags any steps that sound plausible but were not explicitly provided.

artifact.label: Template
artifact.title: Context block
items: Goal: what needs to be produced

doThisNow.task: Pick one active task and build a four-line context block before your next AI prompt.
steps: Add the audience and expected format.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to say what source is authoritative.

roleVariants.manager: Frame AI requests around a deliverable, risk level, and reviewer before you ask for output.
roleVariants.writer: Use AI as a drafting and editing partner, then review for voice, facts, and audience fit.
roleVariants.analyst: Treat AI as a hypothesis generator and explainer, not as a trusted source of record.
roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

lessons.sequenceRationale: After context design, the learner needs a realistic view of why outputs vary so they can stabilize quality without chasing magic prompts.
lessons.title: Why Output Varies
lessons.subtitle: Different outputs from the same request do not mean the system is broken.
lessons.objective: Set realistic expectations for variability and learn when to rerun, refine, or reset a conversation.
lessons.useWhen: Different outputs from the same request do not mean the system is broken.
lessons.whyItMatters: AI is probabilistic. Understanding that helps you stop chasing a perfect single prompt and instead manage the workflow around the model.

coreConcepts.title: Variation Is Normal
coreConcepts.text: Small wording shifts, model updates, and conversation history can change results. That is expected behavior, not necessarily a failure.

coreConcepts.title: Threads Accumulate Baggage
coreConcepts.text: Long chats often mix old assumptions with new requests. Starting a clean thread is often faster than arguing with stale context.

coreConcepts.title: Stability Comes From Structure
coreConcepts.text: Examples, explicit rubrics, and clear output formats make results more consistent than vague requests for the "best answer."

workedExample.scenario: A writer gets three noticeably different summaries from the same document.
workedExample.approach: They create a rubric with audience, length, sections, and source-only requirements.
workedExample.result: The summaries become far more stable because the expected structure is fixed.
workedExample.verification: The writer compares each summary against the rubric instead of comparing vibes alone.

doThisNow.task: Rewrite one prompt as a rubric-based request and rerun it in a clean thread.
steps: Compare the new result to your old version.

commonMistakes: Assuming one great output means the prompt is production-ready.
Continuing a noisy thread too long.

roleVariants.manager: Frame AI requests around a deliverable, risk level, and reviewer before you ask for output.
roleVariants.writer: Use AI as a drafting and editing partner, then review for voice, facts, and audience fit.
roleVariants.analyst: Treat AI as a hypothesis generator and explainer, not as a trusted source of record.
roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson turns the mental model into risk awareness by focusing on where fluent output becomes dangerous without verification.
lessons.title: Hallucinations and Overreach
lessons.subtitle: Fluent language can hide weak evidence.
lessons.objective: Recognize when AI is inventing, stretching, or blending information so you can verify the right things.
lessons.useWhen: Fluent language can hide weak evidence.
lessons.whyItMatters: The harm usually comes from confident specifics: dates, names, citations, causal claims, and policy statements that sound reasonable but were not grounded in source material.

coreConcepts.title: High-Risk Claims
coreConcepts.text: Factual statements, recommendations with business consequences, and anything that cites a source need more scrutiny than simple drafting assistance.

coreConcepts.title: Source Grounding Reduces Drift
coreConcepts.text: When you require the model to use supplied material and call out uncertainty, invented details decrease.

coreConcepts.title: Verification Is Part Of The Task
coreConcepts.text: Do not treat review as optional cleanup. It is part of the workflow whenever the output could influence a decision.

workedExample.scenario: An analyst asks AI for a competitor feature comparison and receives several unsupported claims.
workedExample.approach: They rerun the task using pasted source excerpts and a "say unknown if not stated" rule.
workedExample.result: The second output is narrower but more trustworthy.
workedExample.verification: The analyst spot-checks every feature claim against the source notes before sharing.

artifact.label: Verification
artifact.title: Claim check sequence
items: Mark which source supports each one.
Replace unsupported claims with "unknown" or a follow-up task.

doThisNow.task: Take one AI output you planned to use today and run a simple claim check on it.
steps: Find the source for each fact.

commonMistakes: Asking for certainty when the evidence is incomplete.

resources.title: NIST AI RMF Playbook
resources.note: Practical reference for governance, measurement, and risk-management language that stays more durable than vendor marketing.

lessons.sequenceRationale: The module closes by translating the earlier concepts into a repeatable daily operating pattern the learner can use immediately on live work.
lessons.title: A Daily Operating Pattern
lessons.subtitle: Use AI with the same lightweight sequence every time.
lessons.objective: Build a repeatable loop for safe, efficient AI use on common business tasks.
lessons.useWhen: Use AI with the same lightweight sequence every time.
lessons.whyItMatters: Consistency matters more than prompt cleverness. A simple operating pattern prevents avoidable errors and keeps the tool useful under time pressure.

coreConcepts.title: Frame
coreConcepts.text: Define the task, audience, and constraints before you prompt.

coreConcepts.title: Generate
coreConcepts.text: Ask for a first pass with an explicit structure.

coreConcepts.title: Review
coreConcepts.text: Verify claims, adjust tone, and turn the output into a final work product.

workedExample.scenario: A project coordinator needs a vendor follow-up note, meeting summary, and task list.
workedExample.approach: They run the same three-step loop for each output instead of improvising every time.
workedExample.result: The work is faster and more consistent because the operating pattern stays fixed even when the task changes.
workedExample.verification: Before sending, they confirm names, dates, and owner assignments.

doThisNow.task: Build a one-page AI operating checklist for one live task you own this week.
steps: Name the deliverable and the source of truth.
Write the three checks you must do before sharing output.
Save the checklist where you will see it during the next run of that task.

commonMistakes: Skipping the review step when you are rushed.
Changing your approach completely for every task.
Using AI before clarifying the goal.

roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

outcomes: Build a durable AI mental model.
Learn how to review output instead of trusting fluency.
nextModuleBridge: Next, turn that mental model into better tool choices so you stop forcing one product to do every job.
